schools.summary: Paul introduces the great theme of chapters 8–9 (the collection for the poor saints) by making known 'the grace of God bestowed on the churches of Macedonia' — the liberality of the Macedonian believers being itself a gift of God's grace, not merely human kindness but a work of God's grace in them, that they should give so freely; the giving spirit is God's gift (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): Paul holds up the Macedonians' example to stir the Corinthians to the same liberality — true generosity being a grace God works in the heart.

themes: we do you to wit of the grace of God bestowed on the churches of Macedonia
the grace of God in the Macedonian churches' giving
liberality as a gift of grace
the Macedonian example

schools.summary: The Macedonians gave amid 'a great trial of affliction' — severe persecution and hardship; their generosity came not from ease but from the midst of suffering; and the striking combination — 'the abundance of their joy' (spiritual gladness) and their 'deep poverty' (extreme material want) together 'abounded unto the riches of their liberality' — their joy and even their poverty overflowed in rich generosity (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): that the deeply poor, in affliction, should give richly and joyfully is the marvel of grace; their want did not hinder but heightened their bounty.

themes: in a great trial of affliction the abundance of their joy and their deep poverty abounded unto the riches of their liberality
liberality out of affliction and poverty
joyful giving from want
the marvel of grace in giving

schools.summary: Paul testifies ('I bear record') that the Macedonians gave 'to their power' (up to the full extent of their means) 'and beyond their power' — even more than could reasonably be expected of their poverty — and 'of themselves' (of their own accord, freely, spontaneously), not under pressure or persuasion but from their own willing hearts; the giving was voluntary (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the Macedonians needed no urging but pressed forward of themselves, giving beyond their means — a rebuke to grudging or reluctant giving.

themes: to their power, yea, and beyond their power they were willing of themselves
giving beyond their power, of their own accord
voluntary, spontaneous generosity
a model of free giving

schools.summary: The remarkable detail: the Macedonians, far from needing to be asked, 'prayed us with much intreaty' (earnestly begged Paul) that he 'would receive the gift' — they pleaded for the privilege of giving — and sought 'the fellowship (koinonia, participation, partnership) of the ministering to the saints,' counting it an honour and a grace to have a share in relieving the poor believers (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley (with Calvin): the Macedonians regarded the opportunity to give as a favour, not a burden; they entreated for the chance to help.

themes: Praying us with much intreaty that we would receive the gift
the fellowship of the ministering to the saints
begging to share in the ministry to the saints
giving counted a privilege

schools.summary: The Macedonians acted 'not as we hoped' — they exceeded Paul's expectations — for 'first' they 'gave their own selves to the Lord,' a self-dedication deeper than the gift of money; the right order — they gave themselves first to the Lord (in entire consecration) and then 'unto us' (to Paul, as his fellow-workers in the cause) 'by the will of God,' the gift of substance flowing from the prior gift of self (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the giving of self to God is the root of all true liberality; once the heart is wholly the Lord's, the offering of money follows freely.

themes: first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto us by the will of God
the Macedonians first giving themselves to the Lord
self-dedication deeper than money
self-surrender the spring of giving

schools.summary: Moved by the Macedonian example, Paul 'desired (urged) Titus' — who had earlier 'begun' the collection among the Corinthians — to return and 'finish (complete) in you the same grace also'; Paul calls the collection 'this grace' (the gracious work of giving), and wished Titus to bring it to completion among the Corinthians, who had made an earlier start (a year before, v.10), as he had begun it (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: we desired Titus, that as he had begun, so he would also finish in you the same grace
Titus sent to complete the collection
the gracious work of giving
bringing the collection to completion

schools.summary: Paul lists the graces in which the Corinthians excelled: 'faith,' 'utterance' (speech, eloquence), 'knowledge,' 'all diligence' (earnestness), and 'your love to us' — a richly gifted church; and exhorts that, since they abounded in so many graces, they 'abound in this grace also' (the grace of liberality), their giving matching their other excellences (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Henry): liberality is a grace to be cultivated like faith, knowledge, and love; a church rich in gifts should not be poor in generosity.

themes: as ye abound in every thing, in faith, and utterance, and knowledge... see that ye abound in this grace also
abounding in the grace of giving
liberality a grace like the rest
giving matching other excellences

schools.summary: Paul carefully says 'I speak not by commandment' — he does not lay it on them as an authoritative order, for giving must be voluntary, not coerced; he appeals instead to 'the forwardness (eagerness, zeal) of others' (the Macedonians' example) to stir the Corinthians by comparison, not compulsion; and his purpose is 'to prove (test) the sincerity (genuineness) of your love,' for liberality is a true test of love — willing generosity shows love to be real (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: I speak not by commandment, but by occasion of the forwardness of others
to prove the sincerity of your love
urging giving not by command but to test love
liberality a test of love

schools.summary: Christ 'was rich' in his pre-existent divine glory and as Lord and Maker of all things (the proof of his pre-existence, for he became poor — he had been rich, yet not on earth, where he was always poor; cf. Philippians 2:6-7), and 'became poor' by taking human nature in the incarnation (emptying himself) and choosing a life and death of poverty; the purpose being 'that ye through his poverty might be rich' — rich in the gospel's blessings, God's favour, and eternal glory; his self-impoverishing the means of our enrichment, and the supreme motive to liberality (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin: Christ, because he was God, had all things under his power and authority, and even in his human nature was Lord of all; he became poor by refraining from possessing, giving up his right for a time, and so consecrated poverty in his own person, that believers may not regard it with horror but freely lay out their abundance on their brethren.

Clarke (with Wesley): presses the argument against the Socinians — if Christ were only a mere man, in what sense could he be said to be rich? his family and life were poor throughout; and how could one poor man's poverty make a multitude rich? His pre-existent riches, deity, and the enriching grace of his Spirit are necessary to the sense; questions the Socinian scheme can never answer.

debates.question: In what sense was Christ 'rich' before he 'became poor'?

sides.position: In his pre-existent divine glory and lordship over all things; he became poor by the incarnation (Philippians 2:6-7) and a life of poverty
'Rich in power and the Holy Ghost' (Socinian) — denying pre-existence; rejected as failing the sense
holders: Socinians (noted and refuted by Barnes, Clarke)

themes: though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor
that ye through his poverty might be rich
the supreme example of Christ's self-impoverishing grace
the riches of Christ and his pre-existence

schools.summary: Paul 'gives advice' (his judgment, not a command) that completing the collection 'is expedient (profitable, fitting) for you' — for their own good and credit; the Corinthians had 'begun before' — a year ago they had been 'forward' (eager) both 'to do' and 'to be willing,' an early and willing start, ahead even of the Macedonians (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: having begun so promptly a year before, it would be to their shame not to finish; Paul reminds them of their own earlier readiness.

themes: herein I give my advice: for this is expedient for you
who have begun before... a year ago
Paul's advice to complete what they began
finishing their early start

schools.summary: Paul urges them now to 'perform the doing of it' (complete the act) — that the work begun be actually finished, not left at good intentions; the principle being that as there had been 'a readiness to will' (a prompt willingness), so let there be 'a performance' (the actual doing) — willing must issue in deed — and this 'out of that which ye have,' according to their means, not beyond (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the prompt willingness must issue in the actual doing; good intention is to be crowned with deed, performed out of what one has.

themes: now therefore perform the doing of it
as there was a readiness to will, so there may be a performance also out of that which ye have
matching willing with performance
will issuing in deed

schools.summary: Paul lays down the governing principle: 'if there be first a willing mind (a ready, cheerful disposition)' — the willing heart is the first and essential consideration; without it no gift is acceptable, and with it any gift is; the gift 'is accepted according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not' — God measures the offering by the giver's means and willingness, not by an amount beyond his ability (the widow's mite, Mark 12:42-44); obligation is proportioned to ability (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted according to that a man hath
not according to that he hath not
the gift accepted by the willing mind
obligation proportioned to ability

schools.summary: Paul guards against misunderstanding: 'I mean not that other men be eased (relieved) and ye burdened (distressed)' — he does not wish the Corinthians impoverished in order that others may live at ease; his concern is for equity, the burden of charity fairly shared, not laid so heavily on the Corinthians that they suffer want while others are comfortable (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: a guard against the fanatics who demand community of goods — God does not require us to reduce ourselves to straits that others may be at ease; the rule is proportionate relief, not the abolition of all distinction of property.

themes: I mean not that other men be eased, and ye burdened
not others eased and the Corinthians burdened
equity in giving
proportionate relief, not self-impoverishment

schools.summary: Paul's rule is 'equality' (a fair proportion, a balancing) — that now the Corinthians' 'abundance' should supply the Jerusalem saints' 'want,' and that, should circumstances reverse, the others' abundance might one day supply the Corinthians' want; a mutual, proportionate sharing whereby no believer is left in want while another has abundance (Barnes, Robertson). Ellicott rebuts the Roman Catholic reading that 'equality' means a transfer of superfluous merits to be drawn on in the hour of death or judgment, calling it reading too much between the lines.

Calvin: 'equality' means the communion of saints — a befitting proportion (as among the members of the body) in which they mutually supply each other according to gifts and necessity, not an equality of condition or an absolute leveling; he opposes the 'fanatics' who would make all things common and so overturn civil order, insisting only that none should starve and none hoard his abundance at others' expense.

debates.question: What does the 'equality' Paul commends mean?

sides.position: A proportionate, mutual supply among the churches (communion of saints) — not a leveling of all property
A forced community of goods (the 'fanatics') — rejected as overturning civil order
A transfer of superfluous merits to be drawn on later (Roman Catholic) — rejected

themes: by an equality, that now at this time your abundance may be a supply for their want
that there may be equality
the principle of equality
mutual supply among the churches

schools.summary: Paul quotes Exodus 16:18 (the gathering of the manna): 'he that gathered much had nothing over, and he that gathered little had no lack' — when measured by the omer, all had enough and none too much; applied as an emblem, as God so ordered the manna that, after sharing, all were supplied and none had a surplus, so among Christians the abundance of some should supply the want of others, that all may have enough (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: as hoarded manna bred worms and stank, so wealth heaped up at the expense of needy brethren is accursed and will perish; God gives abundance not for excess or hoarding but for relieving want, requiring frugality and temperance, that none starve and none hoard at others' expense.

themes: He that had gathered much had nothing over; and he that had gathered little had no lack
the manna as the pattern of equality in supply
abundance supplying want
the warning against hoarding

schools.summary: Paul gives 'thanks to God' who 'put the same earnest care (diligence, zeal) into the heart of Titus for you' — Titus's concern for the Corinthians is ascribed to God as its author; Titus shared Paul's own earnest care, and this is traced to God, who put it in his heart; the zeal of faithful ministers is God's gift (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: every good disposition in Christ's servants, including Titus's care for the church, comes from God, and so calls for thanksgiving to him.

themes: thanks be to God, which put the same earnest care into the heart of Titus for you
Titus's God-given care for the Corinthians
ministerial zeal a gift of God
thanksgiving for faithful servants

schools.summary: Gill: Titus accepted the exhortation (the Macedonians, or Paul moved by their example, besought him to go and finish what he had begun), and not only so but, being more forward, of his own accord he went; Barnes: he was eager to go, beyond the mere exhortation; Paul declaring that Titus accepted the exhortation and, being more forward, went of his own accord — the eager willingness of the messenger (Titus not only consenting when urged to undertake the journey but so zealous that he went of his own accord, his own eager love for the Corinthians outrunning the apostle's request, a model of glad and ready service).

themes: he accepted the exhortation
but being more forward
of his own accord he went unto you
the eager willingness of the messenger

schools.summary: Gill: the identity of this brother is uncertain — some think it was Luke the evangelist, the companion of the apostle in his travels (the Syriac and Ethiopic read 'our brother'); Barnes: it has generally been supposed this anonymous brother was Luke; Paul declaring that they sent with Titus the brother whose praise in the gospel is throughout all the churches — the choosing of a man of proven reputation (Paul joining to Titus a fellow-worker, unnamed but widely thought to be Luke, whose faithful gospel labours had earned him honour in all the churches, so the men entrusted with the collection were of unimpeachable standing, to guard against suspicion).

themes: we have sent with him the brother
whose praise is in the gospel
throughout all the churches
a man of proven reputation

schools.summary: Gill: the brother was not only famous for preaching the gospel but was also appointed by the joint suffrages of the churches (made by the lifting up or stretching out of the hand, as the word signifies), chosen not by a few private persons or a single church only but by several churches; Paul declaring that the brother was chosen by the churches to travel with them in this grace (the collection), administered by them to the glory of the same Lord and the declaration of the givers' ready mind — the open, accountable handling of the gift (the bearer formally elected by the churches, so the money was carried under public appointment, the whole administered for the Lord's glory and as a proof of the givers' willing heart).

themes: chosen of the churches to travel with us
with this grace, which is administered by us
to the glory of the same Lord
declaration of your ready mind

schools.summary: Gill: there is an allusion to mariners, who, when sensible of danger, steer their course another way to shun a rock and secure themselves; so the apostles, being aware of the censorious spirits of some, and to prevent all suspicion of their converting any part of the collection to their own private use, took these precautions; Paul stating that they took these precautions, avoiding any occasion for blame in the large sum administered by them — the careful avoidance of suspicion (Paul deliberately arranging for the collection to be handled by chosen, trusted men, so no one could accuse him of dishonesty in managing so large a contribution, guarding his ministry against even the appearance of corruption).

themes: avoiding this, that no man should blame us
this abundance which is administered by us
the careful avoidance of suspicion
guarding the ministry against appearance of corruption

schools.summary: Gill: 'providing' means premeditating, forecasting or considering beforehand in the mind, things that are good and of good report among men as well as accounted good by God; for professors of religion, especially ministers of the gospel, should be careful not only to exercise a good conscience toward God but so to behave as to obtain and preserve a good name among men; Paul declaring that they provide for honest things, not only in the Lord's sight but also in men's — the double standard of integrity (Paul taking deliberate forethought to do what is right and honourable not only before God, who sees the heart, but also before men, who watch the conduct, so his ministry is clear of reproach in both the divine and human view, his honesty open and visible).

themes: providing for honest things
not only in the sight of the Lord
but also in the sight of men
integrity before both God and men

schools.summary: Gill: this is a third person sent about this business; the apostle conformed to the customs of his nation, or at least it agrees with the Jewish canon which requires three persons for the distribution of alms; Paul declaring that they sent with them a brother, often proved diligent in many things, now much more diligent through his great confidence in the Corinthians — the third trusted delegate (Paul adding a third man, one whose diligence he had tested many times and who was now all the more zealous because of the confidence Paul had expressed in the Corinthians, completing a trio of proven men to carry the gift with full accountability).

themes: we have sent with them our brother
whom we have oftentimes proved diligent
now much more diligent
a trio of proven, trusted men

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle here sums up the characters of each of these persons — as for Titus, he is Paul's partner and fellow-helper concerning them; Paul commending the delegates — Titus as his partner and fellow-helper toward them, and the brethren as messengers (literally 'apostles') of the churches and the glory of Christ — the high honour given to faithful servants (Paul vouching for all three, naming Titus his partner in labour and the others the official representatives of the churches and an honour to Christ himself, so the Corinthians should receive them as men of the highest worth).

themes: he is my partner and fellowhelper concerning you
they are the messengers of the churches
and the glory of Christ
high honour to faithful servants

schools.summary: Gill: this is the conclusion, upon summing up the characters of these messengers — since they were persons of so much note and worth, as they ought to be received with great marks of respect and affection, the Corinthians should take care evidently to make it appear to them, who were sent by the churches; Paul exhorting them to show, before the churches, the proof of their love and of his boasting on their behalf — the call to make their love evident (the Corinthians, by their generous giving and their warm reception of the delegates, to give open and public proof both of the genuineness of their love and of the truth of Paul's confident boasting about them, so their conduct vindicates his praise before the watching churches).

themes: shew ye to them, and before the churches
the proof of your love
and of our boasting on your behalf
vindicating Paul's boast by their conduct
